pneumonia.plain_description: an infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs, sometimes filling them with fluid
pneumonia.what_patient_feels: chest pain when breathing or coughing, fever, chills, cough that may produce phlegm, fatigue, difficulty breathing
pneumonia.what_doctor_looks_for: consolidation or opacity in lung fields, often in one lobe, indicating fluid or infection in the air sacs

urgency_context.Critical: requires prompt medical evaluation and likely antibiotic treatment — should be seen today
urgency_context.Moderate: should be evaluated by a physician within 24 to 48 hours
urgency_context.Low: can be monitored with a scheduled follow-up appointment in the coming days

pleural_effusion.plain_description: a buildup of excess fluid between the layers of tissue that line the lungs and chest cavity
pleural_effusion.what_patient_feels: shortness of breath, chest pain especially when breathing deeply, dry cough, fever if infection is present
pleural_effusion.what_doctor_looks_for: blunting of the costophrenic angle on X-ray, haziness at the lung base, fluid accumulation visible on one or both sides

urgency_context.Critical: large effusion may compress the lung and requires urgent drainage or hospital evaluation
urgency_context.Moderate: needs physician evaluation soon to determine underlying cause such as heart failure or infection
urgency_context.Low: small effusion can be monitored with imaging follow-up and outpatient workup

atelectasis.plain_description: a condition where part or all of a lung collapses or does not inflate properly
atelectasis.what_patient_feels: shortness of breath, rapid shallow breathing, cough, low oxygen levels in some cases — mild cases may have no symptoms
atelectasis.what_doctor_looks_for: increased density or opacity in part of the lung, shift of the trachea or heart toward the affected side, loss of lung volume on one side

urgency_context.Critical: large or complete lung collapse requires urgent intervention to restore breathing
urgency_context.Moderate: partial collapse needs physician evaluation and may require breathing exercises or bronchoscopy
urgency_context.Low: minor atelectasis is common after procedures and often resolves with deep breathing exercises and monitoring

cardiomegaly.plain_description: an enlarged heart, which is not a disease itself but a sign of an underlying condition such as high blood pressure or heart disease
cardiomegaly.what_patient_feels: shortness of breath especially when lying down, swelling in the legs or ankles, fatigue, irregular heartbeat, dizziness
cardiomegaly.what_doctor_looks_for: cardiac silhouette wider than half the chest width on X-ray, indicating the heart is larger than normal

urgency_context.Critical: severe enlargement with symptoms of heart failure requires urgent cardiology evaluation
urgency_context.Moderate: should be evaluated by a physician with an echocardiogram to determine the underlying cause
urgency_context.Low: mild enlargement without acute symptoms can be followed up with a scheduled cardiology appointment

no_finding.plain_description: no significant abnormality detected in the chest X-ray at this time
no_finding.what_patient_feels: this result does not indicate any specific symptoms — the scan appears within normal limits
no_finding.what_doctor_looks_for: clear lung fields, normal heart size, no consolidation, no effusion, no masses detected

urgency_context.Critical: no critical findings — result appears normal
urgency_context.Moderate: no significant findings — routine follow-up as directed by your physician
urgency_context.Low: no abnormalities detected — no immediate action required
